beats.activity: Wake up, shower, and prepare for the day
beats.reasoning: Henry can't start the day without a swim, so he's up early. Yesterday's low energy (40/100) suggests he needs this routine reset. Morning shower and prep before heading to the pool.

beats.activity: Commute to Beacon Hill Pool for morning swim
beats.reasoning: Short walk from home; rain is steady but that won't stop a dedicated swimmer. Beacon Hill Pool is his neighborhood spot and essential to his morning ritual.
Full hour for his essential morning swim—the water and exercise will reset his mood and energy after yesterday's low. Rain outside won't matter; he'll be indoors.

beats.activity: Commute to First Hill school, avoiding SR-520 closure via I-90
beats.reasoning: SR-520 eastbound is closed 9am–noon; Henry is heading west from Beacon Hill to First Hill so I-90 is clear. Budgeting extra 10 min for wet conditions and caution. He'll arrive before the closure impacts downtown.

Morning block of counseling work. Rain continues until 2pm, so he stays indoors at school. This is his core work time with back-to-back sessions like yesterday, but the low energy suggests he might be slightly tired—still professional but ready for a break by noon.

beats.activity: Lunch break—grab oat milk latte and sandwich at nearby café
beats.reasoning: His usual spot from yesterday; frugal nature means he sticks with familiar, affordable cafés. Rain is still steady at noon, so staying near work rather than wandering. Quick meal to fuel the afternoon.

beats.activity: Afternoon paperwork, case notes, and final counseling sessions
beats.reasoning: Afternoon work block. Rain clears by 2pm, so the weather improves slightly during this period. Henry wraps up his school day by mid-afternoon to allow time for his garden and evening plans.
Direct commute home. Rain has cleared by now; 40-minute window for typical cross-town timing and Seattle traffic. He's heading back to his neighborhood.

beats.activity: Work in the community garden; tend tomato plants and other beds
beats.reasoning: Yesterday the garden was calling and his tomatoes are thriving; with rain cleared and energy recovering post-swim, he'll spend quality time here. Frugal nature and urban gardening passion align perfectly. Close to home.

beats.activity: Return home, freshen up before evening class
beats.reasoning: Quick freshen-up and change before salsa class. Garden work done; he's at home and needs to prepare for his social/exercise evening activity.

beats.activity: Commute to salsa class in Capitol Hill
beats.reasoning: Beacon Hill to Capitol Hill is a standard cross-town drive (30 min). He values close friends and dancing; Miguel's class is a regular social/exercise outlet. Evening is clear and dry.

beats.activity: Salsa class with Miguel and friends
beats.reasoning: 90-minute class (typical for salsa studios). Yesterday he nailed the footwork; today he's building on that momentum with his close dance community. This is his wind-down activity before heading home.
Evening drive back home from Capitol Hill. 40 minutes for typical traffic and distance. He's now heading back to settle in for the evening.

beats.activity: Wind down at home—light snack, read, relax before bed
beats.reasoning: Evening leisure at home. Henry is back from class and settling in. Frugal nature means he makes a light snack at home rather than eating out. This prepares him for sleep and ensures he's home before the final sleep beat.
7-hour sleep after a full, active day. Yesterday's low energy (40/100) has been recharged by the morning swim, work, garden time, and salsa dancing. He'll wake refreshed tomorrow.

diary: Woke up to grey skies and rain—typical Seattle Tuesday. The pool was quiet this morning, which I needed after a rough weekend. Water always clears my head. Got to school fine; the SR-520 closure had everyone freaked out, but I took I-90 and avoided the worst of it. Three back-to-back sessions with kids dealing with end-of-year anxiety. One third-grader in particular—Marcus—really opened up about his parents' divorce. Those conversations stick with me. Grabbed my usual oat milk latte at Café Solstice and tried to decompress. The garden beds looked thirsty despite the rain, so I spent the afternoon getting my hands dirty with the tomatoes. There's something grounding about that. Salsa class with Miguel was exactly what I needed to shake off the day's heaviness. We laughed through a few stumbles. Heading home now, feeling calmer than I have in weeks.
thought_process: The rain put me in a reflective mood from the start, so I prioritized the morning swim—non-negotiable for my mental health. Work was emotionally demanding; Marcus's situation triggered some old stuff for me, which made me lean harder into the garden afterward. I could've skipped salsa, but I knew isolation would spiral me deeper. Miguel's energy was infectious, and movement helped metabolize the day's heaviness. The SR-520 closure barely affected me since I'd already planned the I-90 route, but it reinforced how fragile systems are. Overall, I felt the weather's dampness trying to pull me down, so I counteracted it with structure and community—swim, meaningful work, plants, dancing, friends. It worked.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
